episodes.title: Toast: Sticky SaaS
episodes.displaySubtitle: Sean Barrett · Founder & CIO, Counter Global · Sticky SaaS
episodes.conclusion: Barrett frames Toast as the restaurant operating system, not a payments terminal with software attached. Purpose-built hardware, seven-module depth, and payments attach drive ~$10,000 of gross profit per location while the stock still prices like a mature name despite winning roughly half of new U.S. openings.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Barrett owns Toast as a core holding: ~25% gross-profit growth, ~35% EBITDA margins, and ~50% share of new openings against a $10 billion gross-profit TAM, yet the stock trades near 18x 2027 GAAP earnings like a mature compounder.

Square competes on payments and SMB tools, but Barrett argues Toast's restaurant-specific hardware and seven-module depth create switching costs Square's generalist stack has not matched in full-service dining.

episodes.title: Curing Cancer? CAR-T Cell Therapy — Science and Business
episodes.displaySubtitle: Curing Cancer? CAR-T Cell Therapy — Science and Business
episodes.conclusion: David's CAR-T session with CARsgen CEO Li Zonghai and oncologist Qi Changsong shows efficacy is proven but economics are supply-constrained: annual output of roughly one to two thousand doses meets only a sliver of six-figure late-line demand in China — physicians, not price alone, ration scarce slots. Investors should model capacity ramps, patient-selection biomarkers, and NRDL reimbursement together; 2171.HK is a bet on manufacturing scale-up and payer access, not science headlines.

investmentIdeas.thesis: CARsgen (2171.HK): CAR-T efficacy proven but ~1–2k annual dose cap vs massive patient pool — upside tied to capacity ramp and NRDL reimbursement, not science alone
Eli Lilly (LLY): scaled CAR-T/oncology platforms post acquisitions — manufacturing and payer muscle advantage in cell-therapy economics versus China pure-plays

episodes.title: The Communist Capitalist Who Saved Investors a Trillion Dollars
episodes.displaySubtitle: The Communist Capitalist Who Saved Investors a Trillion Dollars · Season Spring 2026 · Episode 3
episodes.conclusion: Most Americans already hold Vanguard products — through a 401(k), target-date fund, or broad market ETF — without paying much attention to who runs the firm. That indifference is itself the story. Bogle did not win by stock-picking; he restructured who captured fund-industry economics. Fired from Wellington in 1974 but still chairman of the fund boards, he built a mutually owned manager with no outside shareholders. Fee savings flow back to customers as lower expense ratios rather than dividends. The 1976 index fund raised only $11.3M and took years to gain traction; Vanguard now manages ~$12T at 0.07% average fees. Ben and David estimate roughly $1T shifted from Wall Street to savers. The longer-term tension: Vanguard is among the largest shareholders of corporate America, which raises governance questions the fee narrative alone does not settle.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (~0.03% expense ratio) captures the episode's core idea: US equity beta at minimal cost inside a mutually owned structure where fee reductions accrue directly to holders. Reasonable default core allocation for long-horizon savers who accept market returns minus small drag.

BlackRock is the other passive giant — iShares leads ETFs with ~$3.3T AUM and 1,400 products versus Vanguard's narrower lineup — while Aladdin and institutional diversification create a different but equally durable moat; Salim Ramji's move from iShares to Vanguard CEO BlackRock's ETF dominance as the competitive benchmark.

episodes.title: Cycle Thinking in Investing — Lessons from Losing Money
episodes.displaySubtitle: Cycle Thinking in Investing — Lessons from Losing Money
episodes.conclusion: Yongqing joins Wu Xiaobo for 120 minutes on cycle thinking — enterprise lifecycle, industry lifecycle, capital cycle, and sentiment cycle interlock. He maps four loss traps, separates intrinsic value from price, and notes mutual funds peak-weight sectors at tops. Rational valuation must pair with expected return, not narrative heat. BRK.B Long as cycle-discipline compounder; TSM Watch as capital-cycle bellwether through semiconductor capex swings.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B): cycle-discipline compounder — Yongqing Long through enterprise/industry/capital/sentiment stacks; cash optionality at sentiment peaks
Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM): capital-cycle bellwether — fab capex and funding swings drive earnings; Watch for cycle turn alignment with rational expected-return entry

episodes.title: Innovative Drug Post-Mortem: Valuation and BD Logic
episodes.displaySubtitle: Innovative Drug Post-Mortem: Valuation and BD Logic
episodes.conclusion: Yongqing's biotech post-mortem frames innovation drugs as time's enemy — BD premiums and herd GLP-1/ADC trades diverge from Novartis-style discipline. Patent cliffs meet sudden therapy displacement; Buffett studied pharma in 2020 and passed. Biotech fund positioning hit historic lows in Q4 while BeiGene scale (~$200B market cap) towers over smaller peers. Investors should Watch 2171.HK on capacity and payer gates and NVS on capital allocation discipline versus me-too oncology bets.

investmentIdeas.thesis: CARsgen (2171.HK): biotech post-mortem lens — innovative drugs face time/displacement risk; CAR-T upside tied to capacity and NRDL, not BD premium alone
Novartis (NVS): discipline comparator versus GLP-1/ADC herd — Yongqing favors staged capital allocation over narrative modality chasing

episodes.title: PriceSmart: Central America's Costco
episodes.conclusion: PriceSmart exports Sol Price's membership warehouse model to Latin America and the Caribbean with ~61 clubs, ~$5.5 billion revenue, and no direct club-store competition in its markets. ~40% of EBIT comes from upfront membership fees at $45–$90 tiers, insulating earnings from FX volatility. Controlled real-estate expansion, owned distribution centers after four to five clubs per market, and Kirkland sourcing ties make this a slow but durable compounder targeting affluent emerging-market consumers.

investmentIdeas.thesis: No club-store competition across 12 Latin markets, ~40% EBIT from recurring membership, ~90%+ cash conversion, and runway from ~61 to potentially 100+ clubs via Colombia/Chile densification — Sol Price operating culture and Kirkland ties support a slow compounder at ~$5B market cap.

PriceSmart validates the membership warehouse model's exportability and still sources Kirkland product — a lens on Costco's playbook applied where COST chose not to scale, not a direct relative-value short.

episodes.title: Listener Q&A: Macro Shocks, Pop Mart, AI Research Workflows
episodes.displaySubtitle: Listener Q&A: Macro Shocks, Pop Mart, AI Research Workflows
episodes.conclusion: In an 85-minute listener Q&A, Yongqing balances macro/geopolitics noise with discipline: US equities annualized ~15% since 2009 — he still favors Berkshire or index for most allocators. He walks an AI-augmented research workflow — gap analysis on oil at $60, 8x PE, 12–15% expected return — while trimming Pop Mart guidance from 35% to ~20% as the stock fell ~20%; buyback near 189 HKD is a signal. AI cannot replace offline expert networks. 9992.HK Watch; BRK.B Long.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Pop Mart (9992.HK): Watch after guidance cut from 35% toward ~20% and ~20% stock decline — buyback near 189 HKD supportive; IP craft thesis needs refreshed growth proof
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B): Yongqing core holding — ~15% US annualized since 2009 makes index/Berkshire rational default amid macro noise

episodes.title: Pop Mart: Why Did the Stock Fall After RMB 37.1B Revenue? IP, Fashion Risk & PE Debate
episodes.displaySubtitle: Pop Mart: Why Did the Stock Fall After RMB 37.1B Revenue? IP, Fashion Risk & PE Debate
episodes.conclusion: Recorded March 31, 2026, David and Jeff walk through Pop Mart's narrative break: 2025 revenue RMB 37.1B (+184.7%) but net profit RMB 13B rose only 29.3%, and roughly twenty percent 2026 growth guidance felt like a hard brake — shares fell about thirty percent in two days to near 142–143 HKD, market cap below HKD 200B. The question is whether Labubu is durable IP or a fashion cycle, with Disney near fourteen times price-to-earnings as the comp. Until pipeline breadth proves out, position below traditional compounders and use scenarios, not single-point earnings.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Pop Mart (9992.HK): post-guide shock at ~142–143 HKD, market cap below HKD 200B after ~30% two-day drop — ~20% 2026 growth still respectable if IP pipeline breadth sustains premium vs Disney ~14x
Disney (DIS): episode comps Pop Mart to character-IP compounders at ~14x price-to-earnings — park plus media flywheel sets ceiling on pure toy and IP earnings power

episodes.title: Givaudan: The Magic Ingredients
episodes.conclusion: Fassnacht frames Givaudan as the pick-and-shovel leader of a defensive, innovation-driven industry: ~25% global fragrance share, ~7.5 billion Swiss francs of revenue, and sticky cash-cow formulas where flavor or scent is ~1–5% of a brand's cost but drives repurchase. Trading near 23 times free cash flow with ~4% yield, the stock embeds only ~3% FCF growth despite 5–6% organic history and no negative organic year since the 2000 IPO.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Fassnacht argues defensive 5–6% organic growth, ~18% FCF margins, and formula stickiness deserve a quality premium; at ~23 times FCF with ~4% yield and dividend well covered, the market under-prices durability if new management preserves Roche-era discipline.

episodes.title: Yongqing: Value Investing Framework, Capital Cycles & Quality Compounders
episodes.conclusion: Yongqing's Practical Investments framework ties Graham-era price-to-book thinking to today's quality compounders: intrinsic value moves with lifecycle, and capital cycles — not macro headlines — tell you when crowded trades will compress forward returns. Mutual funds peak weight at narrative tops and de-risk on drawdowns, which contrarians can use as timing signals if they tolerate relative underperformance. The actionable rule is buy durable return-on-invested-capital above cost of capital at reasonable prices, and trim when capital floods into a sector; thirty times price-to-earnings only works if roughly twenty-five percent ROIC sustains ten years.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B): archetypal quality compounder and capital allocator — Yongqing's return-on-invested-capital and capital-cycle discipline align with Buffett framework
Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM): semi quality compounder but capital-cycle timing matters — add on utilization dips, trim when capital expenditure to revenue peaks and funds overweight

episodes.title: The Prancing Horse and the Business of Desire
episodes.displaySubtitle: The Prancing Horse and the Business of Desire · Season Spring 2026 · Episode 2
episodes.conclusion: Ferrari produces roughly 14,000 cars per year while mass manufacturers sell comparable volume in hours, yet the company trades at luxury multiples and ranks among the world's most recognized brands. Ben and David treat it as a scarcity-and-desire business more than a conventional automaker. Enzo built the Scuderia and brand mythology decades before scaling road-car sales at age 50. Luca di Montezemolo reversed Fiat's volume push, imported Hermès-style wait lists and delivery rituals, and benefited from Schumacher's five-year F1 run. Marchionne's 2015 IPO repriced Ferrari as a standalone luxury asset rather than a Fiat division. Today the average selling price is ~$500,000, gross margins ~50%, and 81% of new cars go to existing owners. The F1 team has shifted from a net marketing cost toward a profit center under Liberty, but the operating logic is consistent: constrain supply, sustain global desire, and price accordingly.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Public pure-play on ultra-luxury scarcity: ~14K annual units, ~50% gross and ~39% EBITDA margins, F1-linked brand equity, and pricing power mass OEMs cannot match. Trades at luxury multiples (~35× P/E) with management guiding ~5% revenue growth — a deliberate pyramid strategy rather than volume expansion.

Agnelli family holding company retains ~49% Ferrari voting power alongside Piero Ferrari's ~10% stake. Tracks RACE upside without pure-play exposure — the IPO thesis was Ferrari undervalued inside Fiat until spun out and repriced as luxury.

episodes.title: Decoding Designer Toys: Craft, Business, Trade-offs — Pop Mart
episodes.displaySubtitle: Decoding Designer Toys: Craft, Business, Trade-offs — Pop Mart
episodes.conclusion: Kevin Mingliang, a culture-founder who serves Bilibili and Pop Mart IPs including DIMOO and SKULLPANDA, explains why craft discipline separates Pop Mart from pure fashion: manufacturing quality, IP pipeline depth, and founder-led culture trade off against scale economics. Pop Mart must defend collectible craft while scaling blind-box distribution — not become a apparel label. For investors, 9992.HK is a long-watch on IP monetization quality and supply-chain control; DIS Watch as global IP comparator for franchise durability.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Pop Mart (9992.HK): watch craft-led IP (DIMOO, SKULLPANDA) and manufacturing QC for long-term compounders — differentiate from pure fashion; Kevin frames culture-founder pipeline as moat
Disney (DIS): global IP franchise comparator — Pop Mart must build decades-long character equity; DIS illustrates media-park flywheel Pop Mart lacks but aspires toward

episodes.title: Oral History of SpaceX: Ex-Executive Hong Lide on Musk's Hiring, Mega-IPO, Space & AI
episodes.conclusion: Former SpaceX executive Hong Lide separates 2026's capital frenzy from engineering continuity: IPO and xAI chatter is new for allocators, but Mars planning and Starship iteration began after the first Falcon success in 2012–13. SpaceX's edge is manufacturing culture and vertical integration across thousands of components — not slide-deck synergy. Tesla remains the liquid Musk-industrial proxy; pure-play space names need Starship cadence, per-kilogram cost, and Starlink cash flow before mega-IPO scarcity pricing.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Tesla (TSLA): liquid proxy for Musk manufacturing ecosystem — SpaceX IPO narrative may re-rate industrial optionality or distract from auto core; track execution cadence
SpaceX: 2026 mega-IPO reprises optionality — Hong stresses decade-old roadmap lines; underwrite Starship cadence and Starlink cash flow, not xAI slideware alone
Rocket Lab (RKLB): benefits from space capital inflection but lacks SpaceX full-stack moat — backlog quality and launch cadence vs hype-driven multiples

episodes.title: Yang Meng: Anker's Shallow-Sea Strategy, AI Org Revolution & Billion-Token Company Understanding
episodes.conclusion: Yang Meng defends disciplined category selection at scale: Anker Innovations stays in shallow-sea markets — large enough to build a number-one brand, small enough that Apple-scale rivals do not deploy full war chests — and compounds through roughly 1.1–1.5 points of annual gross margin lift rather than revenue spikes. At RMB 60B+ market cap the test is whether adjacent categories repeat return-on-capital discipline. Heavy Amazon reliance remains the main overhang: rising marketplace ads, policy shifts, and private-label competition can erode third-party margins even when Anker wins category leadership — underwrite margin path and channel mix together.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Anker Innovations (300866.SZ): shallow-sea category leadership compounds through ~1.1–1.5pt annual gross margin at RMB 60B+ cap — track adjacent category ROIC, margin path, and Amazon revenue mix alongside platform fee and ad inflation

episodes.title: He Xiaopeng: Killing the Old Robot Stack, Betting on IRON Humanoids & Physical-World AI
episodes.conclusion: He Xiaopeng's message is a capital-allocation reset: XPeng shut down a legacy robot stack that consumed a few tens of billions RMB (not hundreds) because it could not generalize, then concentrated bets on humanoid IRON — roughly twenty percent probability but company-changing upside. Broad AI across autonomy and robotics runs fifteen to twenty percent of the firm; physical-world data alone costs close to RMB ten billion per year, a rigid line item text models do not share. The episode demands valuing XPeng as auto core cash flow plus a costly option and a data moat only multi-scenario survivors can fund.

investmentIdeas.thesis: XPeng (XPEV): auto core plus 15–20% broad AI burn and ~20% IRON option — track quarterly cash flow and GX deliveries; traditional auto price-to-earnings alone misprices the story
Nvidia (NVDA): physical-world AI and robot training lift edge compute demand — chip layer benefits regardless of which OEM wins the humanoid race

episodes.title: Yusen's VC Watch Ep.2: Harness, Next ByteDance, 2026 Opportunities & Stanley Druckenmiller
episodes.conclusion: Yusen, a China Fund VC observer, reframes 2026 after his cautious AI-return call drew early pushback — normal when frontier curves steepen. His investable thesis is not raw models but the Harness layer: sandboxes, checkpoints, and feedback loops that let weaker models finish long tasks. Beating ByteDance inside ByteDance's distribution game remains unlikely; edge sits in orthogonal niches and China hardware supply chains. Public investors should read this as a private-market map — API margins compress while orchestration and workflow data compound.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Harness/orchestration layer — Yusen frames 2026 edge outside raw models; task checkpoints and sandbox compute become pricing power if coding agents scale
Alphabet (GOOGL): model plus harness plus YouTube/Android distribution — coding feedback loops compound; API alone is not the moat
Meta (META): agent sandboxes and Llama ecosystem bets align with harness thesis — execution on long-horizon tasks determines ROI on AI capex

episodes.title: Freda's Investment Notes Ep.2: Tokenmaxxing, Motors in Steam Engines, Relay to Basketball
episodes.conclusion: Lightspeed partner Freda argues the AI investment frame must shift from token volume to tokens-per-task efficiency — raw burn can rise while waste is real, and CFOs who fund coding tools on usage spikes misread productivity. Organizations are still bolting AI onto steam-engine workflows; electrification took decades because factories had to be redesigned, and relay-baton product teams must become small autonomous squads. For investors: underwrite outcome-based pricing and workflow lock-in (coding agents, harness layers), not headline token charts; META and GOOGL capex stays rational only if task efficiency and org redesign follow.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Meta (META): massive AI/token spend only justified if coding agents convert to outcome efficiency — Freda frames tokenmaxing as real; track whether internal coding ROI and pricing model shift follow capex
Alphabet (GOOGL): coding-led reorg under Corey plus Gemini distribution — self-improving coding loop is strategic; tokens per task discipline determines whether capex compounds
Nvidia (NVDA): token demand and AI-training-AI loops sustain compute spend even as per-task efficiency improves — chip layer wins regardless of application winner

episodes.title: Let Me Go a Little Crazy: Training at Anthropic & Gemini, Heroism Is Over
episodes.displaySubtitle: Let Me Go a Little Crazy: Training at Anthropic & Gemini, Heroism Is Over
episodes.conclusion: After training at Anthropic and joining Google's Gemini team in late 2025, Yao Shunyu argues frontier labs no longer fear falling behind on raw capability — they fear picking the wrong product bet. Benchmark gaps among OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google have narrowed; retention now turns on workflow lock-in, distribution, and reliability, not hero narratives. He puts Gemini consumer share at roughly twenty percent and cites Doubao's voice UX as proof regional product fit can win without global IQ leadership. Investors should underwrite commercial paths — coding agents, search/Android funnels, enterprise attach — rather than leaderboard deltas.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Alphabet (GOOGL): Gemini consumer push plus search and Android distribution — Yao frames late-2025 hiring as Google's volume war; ~20% share guess implies room if retention chains
Coding and agent workflow bet leads via Claude Code, but distillation pressure rises as capabilities converge — must prove durable enterprise ARR and lock-in, not benchmark wins
Nvidia (NVDA): multi-frontier training capex persists as model alpha compresses — chip layer benefits regardless of which product bet wins

episodes.title: Agent Survey: Tech History and the OpenClaw Moment with Su Yu
episodes.conclusion: OSU professor Su Yu traces agent history from logical agents through neural agents to today's language agents — his OpenClaw moment, like ChatGPT's, is an interaction breakthrough, not a single algorithm leap. Continual learning and world models remain the bottleneck; he co-founded NeoCognition with a $40M seed to build a universal digital agent. China moves faster on the application layer; Su is a 2025 Sloan Fellow. Investors should watch NeoCognition's agent stack and GOOGL Long as distribution plus research depth compound language-agent workflows.

investmentIdeas.thesis: NeoCognition: $40M seed universal digital agent — Su Yu targets continual learning and world-model bottlenecks; OSU professor plus 2025 Sloan Fellow pedigree
Alphabet (GOOGL): language-agent distribution (Android, Chrome, Workspace) plus DeepMind research — Su's framework favors integrated stack over pure model APIs

episodes.title: 3.5-Hour Interview with Luo Fuli: OpenClaw, Agent Paradigm, Post-Training, Org Flattening
episodes.conclusion: Luo Fuli, ex-Damo/DeepSeek and Xiaomi MiMo post-training lead, argues the industry has entered the post-training era — her OpenClaw moment came between 2am and 6am on first use, when an agent completed real work overnight. She flattens orgs: shared Mac Minis, group chat, no usage KPIs. GPU splits roughly 3:1:1 research/post-train/inference. Many agent frameworks exist but self-evolving agents remain TBD; post-training needs a pre-training worldview. Investors should watch Anthropic's coding post-train flywheel and GOOGL's ability to align org design with inference economics.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Alphabet (GOOGL): post-training era raises GPU split toward RLHF/SFT — must align Gemini agent behavior with cloud inference economics and flat-team dogfooding
Anthropic: post-training flywheel via Claude Code real-world feedback — Luo's framework validates coding agents as post-train killer app

episodes.title: 4-Hour Interview with Hong Letong: AI for Math, Lean, and the Math Canon
episodes.conclusion: Hong Letong argues AI-for-math is entering industrial scale: Action raised a $1.6B Series A to productize Lean-based formal proof — draft outline, formalize, then prove or leave sorries — turning research math into a verifiable pipeline. Math is structural language, not trivia; bounded attention beats infinite context for reliability. Her 2020 graphs-catch-and-proof paper and B Capital fundraising signal venture conviction, with an Ohio State professor joining the team. Investors should watch Action as a harness play on theorem proving; GOOGL benefits if formal math becomes a model capability layer.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Alphabet (GOOGL): formal math and verification as Gemini differentiator — Action's $1.6B raise signals infrastructure demand for trustworthy reasoning
Action: $1.6B Series A on Lean formal-proof pipeline — bounded-attention theorem proving as harness layer; ex-OSU professor hire signals research depth

episodes.title: Global LLM Quarterly Ep.9: Coding as AGI Act II, Silicon Valley Big Three, Models as Next OS
episodes.displaySubtitle: Global LLM Quarterly Ep.9: Coding as AGI Act II, Silicon Valley Big Three, Models as Next OS
episodes.conclusion: Guangmi frames coding as AGI Act II: Sonnet 4.5–4.6 delivered more Q1 capability than all of 2025, with frontier labs seeing 70–80% of new code AI-written and power users burning hundreds of dollars daily in tokens. Anthropic ARR may overtake OpenAI among the top one to two million users; combined frontier ARR could hit $80–100B by year-end and $200B+ next year. Coding remains difficulty 8–9/10 — the hardest real-world task — so models-as-OS and harness layers compound. Investors should overweight Anthropic's coding flywheel and treat GOOGL/MSFT as distribution bets, not assume ~90% knowledge-work automation is priced in.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Anthropic: Sonnet 4.5–4.6 leap and 70–80% AI-written code at frontier labs — ARR may exceed OpenAI among top 1–2M power users; coding flywheel is AGI Act II
Alphabet (GOOGL): models-as-OS race requires coding harness depth plus Android/Cloud distribution — ~100-day leadership rotation means execution speed matters
Microsoft (MSFT): Copilot distribution scale versus pure-play coding labs — must convert seat count to token-depth flywheels or risk OS-layer commoditization

episodes.title: From Bankrupt Teams to A Global Sports Empire
episodes.displaySubtitle: From Bankrupt Teams to A Global Sports Empire · Season Spring 2026 · Episode 1
episodes.conclusion: Formula 1 combines three distinct competitions: on-track racing, engineering spending at scale, and owner-level politics in the paddock. For decades constructors lost money while Bernie Ecclestone consolidated commercial power over race fees, broadcast rights, and promoter deals — often through structures he controlled without clear legal ownership. Liberty acquired the sport for ~$8B enterprise value, replaced Bernie in 2017, and imposed a $145M cost cap alongside a more professional commercial operation. Drive to Survive expanded the US audience because American viewers lacked inherited F1 context; human narratives could attract new fans without alienating European purists. Average team valuation is now ~$3.6B. The episode's investment framing favors the commercial-rights holder over individual teams: the margin pool sits in media, sponsorship, and promoter economics rather than constructor P&Ls.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Liberty Formula One Group holds Formula 1's commercial rights. After spinning out other assets, FWONK represents the operating entity exposed to media, sponsorship, and promoter economics — and to the turnaround that helped raise average team valuations toward ~$3.6B.

episodes.title: Cloudflare: Leading Cybersecurity
episodes.displaySubtitle: Sam Eden · Investor, Square Peg Global Tech Fund · Leading Cybersecurity
episodes.guestRole: Investor, Square Peg Global Tech Fund
episodes.conclusion: Eden frames Cloudflare as a single global network that gets stronger as it scales: 20%+ of web traffic, 2.5 million attacks blocked per second, and three product acts stacking on shared commodity hardware. Pool-of-funds bundling, channel partners, and edge AI inference extend the moat, though valuation near 25x NTM revenue leaves little room for execution slips.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Eden sees strengthening position: 29%–30%+ sustained revenue growth, multiple levers across Acts 1–3, channel runway from 30% toward peers' ~90%, and a network-effect moat that survived process-error outages transparently — but 25x NTM revenue and capital intensity demand flawless execution to earn returns.

episodes.title: The NFL
episodes.conclusion: The NFL is less a sports league than a century-long exercise in cooperative capitalism. Owners share national media revenue equally, cap player costs as a fixed percentage of that pool, and engineer competitive balance through reverse-order drafts and strength-of-schedule — producing a product where any team can win on any given Sunday. Television transformed ticket-dependent barnstormers into America's dominant media property; the 2023–2033 rights package alone exceeds $112 billion. Ben and David frame the league as communist capitalism among billionaires — rivals who chose to grow the pie together. The 2026 remaster adds private equity minority stakes with league-level carry, Taylor Swift-driven demographic expansion, and Forbes franchise valuations averaging $7.1 billion. The bear case remains revenue inequality across markets, but structurally the NFL remains the most perfectly designed American sports media business.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Disney's pending acquisition of NFL Network and fantasy app in exchange for ~10% of ESPN equity ties the largest sports cable brand to the NFL for a generation — a bet on NFL content as streaming anchor tenant.
Amazon's Thursday Night Football and reported $100M+ New Heights podcast deal validate tech platforms as incremental NFL distribution beyond traditional networks.

episodes.title: Databricks: From Data to Decisions
episodes.displaySubtitle: Alan Tu · Portfolio Manager & Analyst, WCM Investment Management · From Data to Decisions
episodes.guestRole: Portfolio Manager & Analyst, WCM Investment Management
episodes.conclusion: Tu frames Databricks as the data-processing layer enterprises must fix before AI works: $4B+ ARR, >140% net expansion, and a $1B lakehouse product dwarf Snowflake's upstream push. WCM's December 2024 stake reflects platform proof across AI and core data engineering, not a single Spark feature.

investmentIdeas.thesis: WCM's December 2024 investment reflects a platform shift: $4B+ ARR, >140% net dollar expansion, ~$1B AI revenue, and a ~$1B lakehouse product with FCF positivity at ~$130B valuation—Tu sees multiple ways to win whether AI hype rises or fades, including AI-native labs and enterprise RAG pipelines on the same governed data stack.

Snowflake remains entrenched in warehousing, but Tu says Databricks' downstream structured push toward $1B has outrun Snowflake's upstream data-engineering effort—watch share shifts as lakehouse workloads consolidate.

episodes.title: Rolex
episodes.conclusion: Rolex sells over a million watches annually at ~$13,000 average retail — yet maintains waitlists and secondary-market premiums that pure luxury houses like Hermès cannot replicate at comparable unit volume. Ben and David trace a century from Hans Wilsdorf's 1905 London import business through the Hans Wilsdorf Foundation's 1960 ownership structure, the 1970s quartz crisis that destroyed 85% of Swiss market share, and deliberate restraint on production and advertising that turned scarcity into brand equity. Rolex now captures ~30% of Swiss watch industry revenue — triple Cartier or Omega — while remaining privately held with an estimated $50B+ cash hoard. The 2023 Bucherer acquisition verticalizes retail without abandoning the authorized-dealer model. The paradox: a tool-watch company that became the world's most desired luxury brand by refusing to optimize margins the way a public company would.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Hermès (~€290B market cap, ~60x earnings) is the closest public comp for foundation-like luxury with multi-decade brand compounding — useful benchmark for Rolex's estimated $100B+ private value, though Hermès lacks Rolex's unit volume.
Richemont (Cartier, 7.5% Swiss share) shows the gap: even a strong #2 captures a quarter of Rolex's revenue share — illustrating winner-take-most dynamics in accessible luxury watches.

episodes.title: Costco
episodes.conclusion: Costco is Sol Price's retail philosophy perfected at scale: treat customers as smart, cap markups at 14%, pay employees well ($26/hour versus Walmart $19.50), and let membership fees (~70% of operating income) fund the entire retail business model. Kirkland Signature (~$52B revenue) is the world's largest consumer packaged brand; the $1.50 hot dog combo unchanged for 47 years symbolizes institutional commitment to value over margin extraction. Ben and David trace FedMart → Price Club → Costco merger (1993), showing how negative cash conversion cycles, limited SKUs (~3,700), and 93% US renewal rates compound into ~$230B revenue and ~$7.5B operating income at ~11% gross margins. Charlie Munger's favorite company for good reason — durable advantage compounds through decades of consistent trade-offs, not quarterly optimization.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Costco's ~$230B revenue, 93% renewal, and membership-funded model produce ~$7.5B highly defensible operating income — premium valuation (1.6x revenue) reflects multi-decade compounding at ~8% growth with unmatched trust.

Sam's Club competes on warehouse price but lacks Costco's 14% markup cap culture and Kirkland's ~$52B scale; Walmart's ~$600/sq ft revenue versus Costco's ~$1,800/sq ft and 2M+ versus ~300K employees shows the productivity gap Sam's cannot close by copying format alone.

episodes.title: 10 Years of Acquired (with Michael Lewis)
episodes.conclusion: Ten years in, Ben and David invite Michael Lewis to the Menlo Park garage where Google started to ask why Acquired succeeded where 99% of podcasts fail. The answer blends Berkshire-style circle of competence, Sequoia-like partnership discipline, and Hermès-inspired quality scarcity — only ~15 episodes per year, each consuming a month of research. Lewis reframes their work as narrative non-fiction with a business lens: find obscurity-to-ubiquity hero journeys with secrets hiding in plain sight. The episode meta-analyzes Acquired's sponsor strategy (enterprise deals, not CPM ads), true subscriber distribution without algorithmic disintermediation, and terror-driven quality control. Unlike Lewis's books that create phenomena from obscurity, Acquired covers companies audiences already love — the craft is revealing the machine behind familiar brands.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Spotify's podcast investments compete on volume; Acquired's model suggests durable value accrues to direct-subscriber shows with premium production — a niche within Spotify's broader podcast strategy.

episodes.title: Amadeus: The IT Backbone of Travel
episodes.displaySubtitle: Ben Needham · Portfolio Manager, 91 Asset Management · The IT Backbone of Travel
episodes.conclusion: Needham frames Amadeus as the friendly gorilla of travel IT: ~50% share in distribution and airline IT, inflation-linked 10–15 year contracts, and ~70% contribution margins on air IT. AI-agent fears look overblown while Navitaire order-management upgrades and a peer-weakened hotel IT market offer mid-teens revenue-per-booking upside.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Needham sees inflation-linked air IT (~70% contribution margins, ~50% share), rising net distribution fees through NDC, and Navitaire-driven ARPU uplift against a travel market growing ~1.5–2× GDP — with balance-sheet optionality as levered peers retreat from hotel IT.

Sabre trails at ~600–700 million passengers boarded versus Amadeus's 2 billion-plus and exited hotel IT amid balance-sheet pressure — Needham argues dual IT-plus-distribution scale lets Amadeus underprice and cross-sell while Sabre lacks the same integrated halo.

episodes.title: Doximity: The Hub of Healthcare
episodes.displaySubtitle: Jim Jones · Partner & Analyst, William Blair · The Hub of Healthcare
episodes.conclusion: Jones frames Doximity as a doctor-first workflow platform monetized through pharma ads with measurable ROI — 80% physician penetration, ~90% gross margins, and a long runway as healthcare digital ad spend catches the rest of the economy while AI scribe and clinical-reference tools deepen daily use.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Jones values cash flows on secular healthcare digital shift plus wallet-share gains: 80% physician penetration, 55% EBITDA margins, ~$900M net cash, and measurable pharma ROI support durable earnings power if engagement holds.

episodes.title: Coca-Cola
episodes.conclusion: Coca-Cola is syrup, sugar, and water — yet a ~$300B company serving 2.2 billion drinks daily across a system generating ~$175B in total revenue. Ben and David trace 139 years from John Pemberton through Asa Candler's $2,300 acquisition (1891), the $1-per-gallon bottler contracts that nearly destroyed the company, and the New Coke disaster (~3% share after Classic relaunch). Asset-light franchise — 200 partners, 950 facilities — produces 60% gross margins on $47B concentrate revenue. Munger's $2M-to-$2T thought experiment fails at 3–4% post-1998 growth. Buffett's ~$1.3B stake now ~9.5% worth ~$28B with ~$800M–$1B annual dividends. Brand plus distribution architecture beats product complexity — though sugar headwinds prevent Charlie's Lollapalooza outcome.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Coca-Cola remains a dividend aristocrat with ~$800M–$1B flowing to Berkshire annually — brand moat and 60% concentrate gross margins intact but 3–4% growth limits capital appreciation versus Munger Lollapalooza frame.

Episode frames PepsiCo's Frito-Lay diversification as growth edge over KO's cola concentration — relative underperformance thesis if sugar headwinds persist and KO portfolio brands outperform in developed markets.

episodes.title: GE Aerospace: Full Throttle
episodes.conclusion: Narayanan frames GE Aerospace as a post-spin pure play on a razor-and-blade franchise: ~70,000 engines in service, ~$175 billion backlog, and ~70% of revenue from mandated aftermarket services with ~25% segment margins. Larry Culp's lean turnaround and CFM International's sole-source narrow-body positions support 8–10% commercial-services growth, though OE losses on Leap ramp and multi-decade technology bets on open-rotor architecture remain the long-cycle risks.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Narayanan argues GE Aerospace combines oligopoly scale, ~$175B backlog, and 8–10% commercial-services growth with 20–25% cash returns on capital; post-spin pure-play exposure rewards patience as Leap OE losses fade and aftermarket pricing compounds.

episodes.title: Robinhood: Mobile First, Margins Later
episodes.displaySubtitle: Arthur Olson · Founding Partner, Ravenswood Partners · Mobile First, Margins Later
episodes.conclusion: Olson argues Robinhood is the brokerage of the next generation — 26 million funded accounts, 95% retention, and product velocity that turned a 2022 refounding into nine nine-figure revenue lines. Mobile-first UX plus payment-for-order-flow economics mattered, but the durable edge is demographic share (65%+ Gen Z) and wallet expansion as $10,000 average accounts compound toward banking, Gold, and wealth tools over decades.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Olson sees a multi-decade asset-gathering flywheel: 20% account share at 2% asset share closes as millennials and Gen Z compound balances, banking/Gold add recurring revenue, and ~81% incremental margins on cloud infrastructure support earnings power — tempered by regulatory and valuation risk after a sharp rerating.

episodes.title: Trader Joe’s
episodes.conclusion: Trader Joe's is a ~$24–25B private grocery business doing $2,000+ per square foot — double Whole Foods and 4x industry average — with ~80% private label, deliberately small stores, and cult customer loyalty. Ben and David trace Joe Coulombe's 1967 Pronto Markets buyout ($25,000, employees co-invested), the 1960s 747-driven globalization insight, and 1979 sale to Aldi Nord's Theo Albrecht — who let Joe run it another decade. Two Buck Chuck (Charles Shaw label bought for $27,000; 1B+ bottles sold) exemplifies the model: absolute margin dollars over margin percentages, curated SKUs (~4,000), and crew paid 40–60% above market with ~5–6% turnover. The episode reveals why Aldi-owned Trader Joe's avoids Aldi branding — and why estimated ~$32–35B private valuation rivals 7-Eleven despite no public markets.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Trader Joe's (~$32–35B estimated, Aldi Nord-owned) demonstrates private grocery can compound at ~11% revenue growth without public markets — no direct investment but benchmark for Sprouts/Fresh Market niche players.

episodes.title: Google Part III: The AI Company
episodes.guest: Google Part III: The AI Company
episodes.displaySubtitle: Part III: The AI Company · Season 18 · Episode 1
episodes.conclusion: Google Part III argues the search company was always an AI company — and that buying DeepMind for $550M (2014) may be worth $500B today while indirectly birthing OpenAI. Ben and David trace Brain's cat paper (2012, 10M YouTube frames), the Hinton auction (~$40M+, Baidu $12M opening bid), Transformer authors (2017), and the organizational split between DeepMind London and Google Brain that delayed product integration. DeepMind's acquisition triggered Elon Musk and Sam Altman to found OpenAI (~$1B pledged, 2015) when researchers wanted open publication Google would not allow. Larry approved $130M NVIDIA order when finance objected — NVIDIA revenue ~$4B then. Gemini finally unifies the stacks under Sundar Pichai, but the episode's tension is clear: Google invented the modern AI era yet nearly gave it away through talent exodus and product paralysis between 2013 and 2023. Search holds ~90% share with lock-in; PHIL consumed ~15% of data center capacity by mid-2000s — Google monetized AI internally before ChatGPT without consumer-facing credit.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Google owns Transformer-era IP, DeepMind, TPUs, and distribution — Gemini unification addresses decade of organizational delay; AI monetization on ~$100B+ quarterly revenue base is underpriced if product velocity matches research leadership.

OpenAI partnership gives Microsoft consumer AI brand leadership Google lacked — but Google retains fuller stack (TPU, data, Search); competitive outcome depends on Gemini execution vs. ChatGPT/Copilot distribution.

episodes.title: Exor: Fiat Crisis to Ferrari Glory
episodes.displaySubtitle: Fiat Crisis to Ferrari Glory · Public · EXXRF
episodes.conclusion: Exor is a patient-capital holding company whose NAV per share growth over the past decade came overwhelmingly from Ferrari, while Stellantis, CNH Industrial, and Philips trade through cyclical lows. John Elkann closed a 50–60% NAV discount by trimming Ferrari and buying back stock at roughly €100 versus ~€180 NAV. The bet requires a decade-long horizon and faith that Elkann's people-first, debt-light capital allocation can renew the portfolio beyond racing luxury.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Ferrari drove most of Exor's NAV per share growth and remains the highest-quality asset — ultra-luxury positioning, controlled volume, and EV transition as further proof it is not a mass-market automaker; many Exor holders may prefer direct Ferrari exposure at full multiple.

Stellantis is a pure auto cyclical at ~10–12% of Exor NAV, facing EV transition and overcapacity headwinds; Raj sees reasonable-to-cheap valuation but no moat comparable to Ferrari — useful as a embedded value option inside Exor, not a standalone high-conviction long from this episode.

episodes.title: Mercado Libre: E-commerce Empire
episodes.displaySubtitle: Daniel Wu · Investor, Bristlemoon Capital · E-commerce Empire
episodes.conclusion: Wu frames MELI as Amazon retail plus Alipay in one flywheel: ~$120B market cap, commerce and fintech each compounding, and Brazil at ~55% of revenue. Logistics, ads, and Mercado Credito extend the moat if credit and macro risks stay contained.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Wu sees a dual-engine compounder: ~26% regional e-commerce share, $51.5B GMV, 100M-plus buyers, and fintech cross-sell with 9x revenue growth since 2019—reinvestment funds logistics and Mercado Credito scale in underpenetrated LatAm markets.

Reustle notes MELI's ~$120B market cap was roughly 3x eBay despite starting as the 'eBay of LatAm,' highlighting how commerce-plus-fintech optionality re-rated the business beyond classified marketplace economics—watch whether eBay's stagnant GMV contrasts with MELI's 9x revenue growth since 2019.

episodes.title: Google Part II: Alphabet
episodes.conclusion: Google Part II covers the innovation factory era: Gmail, Maps, Docs, Android, Chrome, and the $1.65B YouTube acquisition that Ben and David regrade from 'terrible' to 'screaming deal.' The 2015 Alphabet reorganization separated Google's cash engine from Other Bets — Waymo, Verily, X — while preserving Larry and Sergey's long-term bets. YouTube consumed more bandwidth than the entire 2000 internet by 2007 and 20% of internet bits by 2014; the 50% creator rev-share delayed profitability a decade but built an ecosystem no competitor matched. Workspace has billions of users but Microsoft's Office segment (~$120B) still dwarfs Google's Cloud segment (~$50B). The episode ends at AI's dawn — Google's moat is logged-in web applications feeding search and ads, with YouTube as the ultimate logged-in engagement flywheel.

investmentIdeas.thesis: YouTube regraded as screaming deal plus Search/Ads cash engine funds AI capex — Alphabet structure preserves Waymo/Cloud optionality while core generates massive quarterly revenue.

Microsoft's ~$120B productivity segment vs. Google's ~$50B cloud segment shows enterprise monetization lead — Workspace growth constrained despite user parity; Azure hybrid story competes with Google Cloud.

episodes.title: The Jamie Dimon Interview
episodes.conclusion: Jamie Dimon tells Acquired's Chase Center audience how he built JPMorgan Chase into a ~$800B fortress after being fired from Citigroup in 1998 — the heir-apparent discarded, restarting at troubled $30B market-cap Bank One in Chicago. He invested $60M of personal capital (half his net worth), fixed risk culture and a 21-person board, then merged into JPMorgan (2004) where Bank One shareholders received 42%. The fortress balance sheet — conservative accounting, excess capital, planning for four-sigma disasters — enabled Bear Stearns and Washington Mutual rescues in 2008 and First Republic in 2023. Dimon cites risk discipline, internal incentive realignment, and diversified businesses feeding each other as separation drivers; a 15-point efficiency ratio advantage compounds reinvestment. At 68 minutes, the interview compresses twenty years into live conversation rather than archival deep dive.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Fortress balance sheet and crisis-acquired scale produce ~15-point efficiency ratio advantage compounding reinvestment — ~$800B market cap reflects durable trust premium, though key-man and regulatory risks persist.

episodes.title: Google Part I: Origins of Search
episodes.guest: Google Part I: Origins of Search
episodes.displaySubtitle: Part I: Origins of Search · Season 17 · Episode 2
episodes.conclusion: Google Part I traces search from Larry Page's 1996 BackRub thesis through PageRank, the $1M Yahoo rejection, Andy Bechtolsheim's $100K uncapped check, and AdWords — the business model that 5x'd revenue in one year (2001: $86M → 2002: $440M). Ben and David show Google won on three axes: relevance (PageRank), scale (distributed crawling on cheap hardware), and monetization (AdWords auction with Ad Rank quality score). Yahoo passed on buying PageRank for $1M; later floated $3B acquisition while Google asked $5B — no deal. The AOL 2002 bake-off committed $100M minimum when Google barely had the cash, validating that superior monetization beats distribution alone. Page and Brin's genius was adopting the best external ideas — Overture's CPC model, not inventing everything — while building infrastructure competitors couldn't match.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Search AdWords economics — born from 2002's $86M-to-$440M transition — still fund Alphabet's AI and Other Bets; PageRank plus infrastructure moat remains durable despite distribution shift to mobile and AI interfaces.

episodes.title: The Steve Ballmer Interview
episodes.conclusion: Steve Ballmer's interview reframes Microsoft Volume II: he claims credit for inventing enterprise sales after IBM divorced Microsoft over OS/2, and argues the DOJ era and flat stock masked a business that tripled revenue and planted Azure. The IBM PC DOS deal — non-exclusive licensing that let Compaq clone the platform — remains the origin of Microsoft's scale; Ballmer raised his hand when Bill Gates did not want to sell to CIOs. The enterprise agreement bundled Windows, Office, Exchange, Active Directory, and SQL Server with honor-system true-ups on zero-marginal-cost software — email pulled the suite. Misses on mobile, search, and social cost trillions elsewhere, but Ballmer holds ~4% of Microsoft (~$140B+) and funds USAFacts philanthropy largely from dividends. Intuit Dome applies the same competitive intensity: $2B privately funded arena, acre-scale halo board, home-court design tuned via analytics.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Ballmer's hold (~4%, ~$140B+) reflects belief that enterprise EA seeds and early Azure bets compound under Nadella; ~$3.5T market cap validates cloud + AI narrative on infrastructure he helped fund — not a consumer-success story but a distribution-and-suite story.

episodes.title: Epic Systems (MyChart)
episodes.conclusion: Epic Systems is a privately held Wisconsin company whose Chronicles single-database architecture powers MyChart for roughly 280M+ patient records and ~45% of Americans. Judy Faulkner raised only $70K equity plus $70K debt in 1979; the company now does ~$5.7B revenue with almost no customer churn. Competitors glued billing and clinical systems via M&A; Epic built Resolute, EpicCare, and hundreds of modules on one database — reliability for hospitals that cannot tolerate data gaps. Policy tailwinds from Medicare/Medicaid (1965) and ARRA HITECH (2009) forced digitization; Epic won Cerner at DOD/VA (~$16B) and dominates academic medical centers. Ben and David estimate $100B+ private value; GE, Microsoft, and Google courted acquisitions that will never close. AI ambient documentation (Microsoft/Nuance) partners through Epic's choke-point role.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Microsoft/Nuance ambient clinical AI reaches hospitals primarily via Epic partnership — Epic's choke point amplifies MSFT healthcare AI TAM without owning the EHR; Nuance DAX adoption through Epic's ~280M patient record install base.
Oracle owns Cerner after ~$28B acquisition but Epic won DOD/VA (~$16B) and dominates large IDNs; Cerner integration risk leaves Oracle playing catch-up in highest-acuity US hospital segment.

episodes.title: Indian Premier League Cricket
episodes.conclusion: The Indian Premier League turned cricket into a franchise sports product by concentrating BCCI commercial rights and borrowing American league design. Lalit Modi raised Sahara's kit sponsorship from ~$100K/year to ~$105M/year (~$1M per match day) within months of joining the board, then re-bid TV rights from ~$10–15M to ~$500M over four years (~$125M/year). IPL's 2008 launch used city franchises (minimum ~$50M over 10 years), player auctions, cheerleaders, and Bollywood ownership to expand beyond Test-match purists to women and children — essential when India's 2008 ad market was only ~$2–3B total. Franchise values reached ~$1B+; media deals escalated to ~$6B for 2023–27. Ben and David frame IPL as Lalit's commercialization of latent demand in a cricket-obsessed nation whose middle class grew from ~2M earning >$10K in the early 1990s to ~550M today.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Central media rights (~$6B 2023–27) and ~$1B+ franchise values reflect India's ad-market growth; no pure-play public equity — watch Disney/Star successor and franchise-adjacent consumer brands benefiting from cricket attention monopoly.

episodes.title: TSMC Founder Morris Chang
episodes.conclusion: Morris Chang's Taipei interview explains TSMC's trillion-dollar outcome as pure-play foundry discipline plus learning-curve pricing. Founded at 56 in 1987 when fabless barely existed, TSMC served IDM overflow before NVIDIA, Qualcomm, and Apple arrived. The 2009 yield crisis never included layoffs; Chang settled NVIDIA's ~$100M dispute over dinner. The 28nm bet (~$6B CapEx in 2010 after 40nm stumbles) coincided with smartphones; Apple forced a 20nm detour (~$10B) before TSMC won all Apple Silicon. Gross margins mid-50s while customers earn 70–80% reflects value split with designers. Hsinchu Science Park ecosystem — ASML, Synopsys, ARM co-located — is unreplicable; Arizona fabs lack leading-edge volume. Semiconductor market grew $26B (1987) to ~$527B (2024); new fabs cost ~$20B heading to $100B — natural monopoly at the leading edge.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Pure-play foundry monopoly at leading edge; AI/GPU demand drives advanced node utilization; ~55% gross margins and learning-curve scale barriers ($20B+ fabs) limit competitors — geopolitical Taiwan risk is primary bear case.
NVIDIA–TSMC partnership since RIVA 128; AI GPU demand depends on TSMC CoWoS/advanced packaging capacity — supply allocation is strategic bottleneck for AI capex cycle.

episodes.title: Mars Inc. (the chocolate story)
episodes.conclusion: Mars Inc. crossed ~$50B sales as a family-owned confectionery and pet-food conglomerate built by Forrest Mars Sr.'s operational obsession. Frank Mars created the Milky Way (~1923); Forrest expanded US and UK businesses, copied European chocolate processes, and bought M&M's partner out for ~$1M (1949) for 20% stake valuing candy at ~$65M today-adjusted. Mars runs 18% return-on-assets targets (~5-year payback), time clocks for all employees including CEOs, and 30% fewer workers than Hershey with higher output per worker. Forrest's 1964 deal swapped Mars stock for Warren Buffett's See's Candies stake — Buffett later called it his worst trade. Pet food (Pedigree, Royal Canin) and Wrigley gum diversified beyond chocolate; company remains private with Mars family control and no public reporting.

investmentIdeas.thesis: HSY public comp for US chocolate; Mars leads globally with superior output/employee and hidden pet diversification at ~$50B private scale.

episodes.title: SpaceX: Rocket Ship
episodes.displaySubtitle: Rocket Ship · Private · Space launch & satellite internet
episodes.conclusion: Ward frames SpaceX as business innovation married to engineering: Falcon 9 cut launch costs roughly 20× versus the shuttle through vertical integration and partial reusability, while Starlink's ~6,000-satellite constellation funds Starship's push toward ~$10/kg launches. The bet is that cost-curve deflation unlocks orbital manufacturing and connectivity markets far larger than today's government-led launch demand.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Ward's Baillie Gifford thesis: integrated cost deflation from Falcon 9 reusability funds Starlink cash flows that underwrite Starship's path to ~$10/kg — opening connectivity and orbital manufacturing TAMs orders of magnitude above today's launch market.

Amazon's Project Kuiper competes for LEO constellation capacity in a supply-constrained launch market; Ward notes SpaceX may open third-party launches but prioritizes internal Starlink cadence where returns fund the Mars/Starship roadmap.

episodes.title: IKEA
episodes.conclusion: IKEA is the world's largest furniture retailer (~€50B+ revenue) built on Ingvar Kamprad's Lista frugality: only ~500 kronor (~$63) ever raised (1938), 100% family ownership until foundation transfer, and €25B+ cash with owned real estate. The 1956 flat-pack LÖVET table discovery — remove legs for transport — unlocked self-assembly, lower breakage, and catalog-showroom hybrid (1953 Älmhult exhibition). Poland sourcing (1960s) and board-on-frame LACK table (~$9.99 today) drove Scandinavian simple design into urbanizing Europe. Germany remains largest market; meatballs (~1B/year) increase dwell time. IKEA lacks a global scaled furniture competitor because flat-pack + owned stores + catalog UX is one integrated system — but e-commerce delivery erodes margin on the model Kamprad optimized for in-store pickup.

investmentIdeas.thesis: No public equity; ~€50B+ private retailer with €25B cash. Watch housing-cycle home-improvement peers — HD correlation is weak versus IKEA's flat-pack integrated model.

episodes.title: Meta
episodes.conclusion: Meta connects ~4B monthly and ~3.3B daily active users across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Threads — more humans than any entity in history (Roman Empire peaked ~40% vs Meta ~50%). Mark Zuckerberg built on LAMP stack and AIM-era hacking culture; Facebook launched February 4, 2004 at Harvard with domain-gated identity and user-submitted photos. Feed ads and mobile pivot (post-2012 HTML5 miss) created one of two great performance-ad systems; US/Canada ARPU rose ~$11 at IPO to ~$227. Apple ATT (2021) erased ~$232B market cap in one day but Advantage+ restored targeting at scale. Reality Labs lost ~$60B since 2019 on AR/VR bet needing iPhone-level profits to break even. Mark's super-voting control enables turn-based strategy: copy Stories/TikTok, buy Instagram/WhatsApp, open-source Llama to commoditize AI complements. Company 'moves like water' — product as discovery, not pure invention.

investmentIdeas.thesis: ~$47B operating income scale; Reels closed TikTok gap; Llama commoditizes AI inputs. Reality Labs ~$60B losses and teen regulation remain key risks under founder control.

episodes.title: Acquired LIVE from Chase Center (with Daniel Ek, Emily Chang, Jensen Huang and Mark Zuckerberg)
episodes.guest: Acquired LIVE from Chase Center (with Daniel Ek, Emily Chang, Jensen Huang and Mark Zuckerberg)
episodes.displaySubtitle: Daniel Ek, Emily Chang, Jensen Huang and Mark Zuckerberg · Season 15 · Episode 1
episodes.conclusion: Acquired's September 2024 Chase Center live show (~6,000 seats, JP Morgan Payments) blended podcast meta-commentary with on-stage updates. Daniel Ek revealed Spotify's podcast chart: organic doubling YoY since 2015, subscriber tailwind from Spotify entering podcasting (2019, market leader in ~3 years), and May 2024 Wall Street Journal article as first-ever 'kink' in the growth curve. Emily Chang grilled Ben/David on YouTube (~$5B revenue at episode time vs ~$35–40B now, 55% creator payout), LinkedIn (~$16B revenue, 5x since 2016 acquisition), SpaceX/Starlink (~$6.5B Starlink revenue), and Taylor Swift Inc (~$550M+ annual free cash flow — Eras tour ~$1.1B gross 2023). Jensen Huang corrected the viral 'enriched us all' clip. Mark Zuckerberg closed Act III on Meta strategy, Ray-Ban Meta glasses, and turn-based product discovery — same themes as the dedicated interview episode.

investmentIdeas.thesis: YouTube ~$35–40B revenue with 55% creator payout still under-earns strategic value to Google — search #2 position, Shorts, and AI training data make non-ownership existential; live show upgrades grade to A+ on optionality not DCF.
LinkedIn ~$16B revenue validates 2016 ~$26B deal; professional graph + AI hiring content moat compounding inside Microsoft.

episodes.title: The Mark Zuckerberg Interview
episodes.conclusion: The standalone Mark Zuckerberg Chase Center interview (~89 minutes) covers Meta's survival across nine 'death' narratives (MySpace through ChatGPT). Mark describes product creation as discovery chiseled from marble — Stories copied from Snapchat, engineering speed supplies turns. Built on LAMP stack; now largest modern open-source beneficiary via Llama. Mobile HTML5 at 2012 IPO caused ~50% drawdown before native + feed ads. Super-voting enables 20-year horizon — '20 years isn't that long.' Reality Labs spend is platform-independence bet like Blue Origin inside Amazon. Ray-Ban Meta glasses shipped pre-LLM; Mark called Saturday highway to add on-device Meta AI. Meta remains Mark amplifier — misunderstood vs Jobs/Ives or Jensen dynamics.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Founder control + ~3.3B DAU + Llama hedge on AI supply; RL drag capped by Mark's commitment — interview confirms strategic patience beyond quarterly optics.

episodes.title: Microsoft Volume II
episodes.conclusion: Microsoft Volume II covers 1995–2014: Internet tidal wave, browser wars, antitrust breakup order, and Ballmer CEO era that tripled revenue while stock flatlined. Netscape IPO Aug 1995 at ~$3B cap; IE bundled free Dec 1995 — Netscape stock dropped ~33% and never recovered. Judge Jackson Nov 1999 found Windows monopoly; June 2000 ordered breakup — market cap fell ~$600B to ~$270B (~55%) before settlement. Steven Sinofsky's 1994 Cornell snowstorm memo sparked Bill's internet religion; Jay Allard's Jan 1994 memo laid embrace-extend strategy before Netscape existed. Ballmer inherited ~80x earnings peak, DOJ trauma, options mess — built Azure via Ray Ozzie skunkworks outside Server & Tools. Satya Feb 2014 CEO; MSFT ~$465/share, ~$3.5T cap ten years later with Azure ~half value. Ben argues 'lost decade' narrative ignores enterprise tripling and cloud seed — Satya fixed storytelling not fundamentals.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Volume II proves antitrust survived, enterprise EA moat funded Azure seed, Satya unlocked narrative on Ballmer fundamentals. ~$3.5T cap pays for cloud plus Office lock-in, not consumer wins.
Netscape/browser war winner of platform layer post-PC; MSFT failed to contain search/mobile — Google captured post-IE platform economics MSFT feared from Netscape.

episodes.title: Starbucks (with Howard Schultz)
episodes.conclusion: Howard Schultz joins Ben and David for the full Starbucks story: three founders sold beans; Howard's 1983 Milan trip inspired the café model; Il Giornale raised $1.6M, bought six Starbucks stores for $3.8M (1987) with Bill Gates Sr. helping remove a blocking investor. The model — ~80% gross margin, high-frequency low-ticket visits, customization, names on cups — scaled globally (~39,000 stores, 86 countries). Partner benefits (1988 health for part-timers, Bean Stock) and 'affordable luxury' cups as billboards built humanity at scale. Frappuccino (1995 Coffee Connection acquisition), Japan (1996 cold-call Kay Nishi parallel to Microsoft), China (Belinda Wong), mobile order (~33% transactions, ~$1.8B prepaid float) followed. 2008 crisis: seven months from insolvency, store closures, retraining 135,000 partners — profits $315M to $945M by 2010. Founder-led risk: succession remains unsettled in 2024 as Howard reflects on scale versus soul.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Mature global footprint with mobile float (~$1.8B) and China exposure (~18% revenue) — partner culture and ~80% gross margins create durable moat; monitor same-store recovery and succession stability post-Howard interim era.

episodes.title: Microsoft Volume I
episodes.conclusion: Microsoft Volume I traces Bill Gates and Paul Allen from Lakeside School PDP-10 access (1968) to the IBM PC deal that created modern software economics. Born 1955 alongside the microprocessor wave, they wrote Altair BASIC without a finished product, licensed to MITS ($180K lifetime cap — mistake), won 1977 arbitration, then priced BASIC at ~$30 to every OEM to kill rivals — Apple paid $31,000. IBM Project Chess (1980) needed DOS; Gary Kildall missed the meeting flying his plane; Microsoft bought QDOS (~$50K–$75K), licensed non-exclusively to IBM while retaining OEM freedom — transferring IBM's dominance into Microsoft's. Mary Gates' board network and Bill Sr.'s law firm shaped negotiations young Bill 'constantly underestimated' leveraged. Steve Ballmer joined 1980; Simonyi brought applications vision. Positive spiral: lower price, more adoption, more software, stronger standard. Part I ends pre-Windows dominance — splinter moment: IBM played checkers, Gates played 3D chess.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Volume I seeds still visible: platform toll-taking, enterprise trust, international DNA — today's Azure/OpenAI extend same positive-spiral logic; Part II/III cover applications and cloud moats built on DOS origin.

episodes.title: Intuitive Surgical: Robotic Precision
episodes.displaySubtitle: Joe Thomas · Analyst · 91 Capital · Robotic Precision
episodes.conclusion: Joe Thomas of 91 Capital frames Intuitive Surgical at roughly $140 billion market cap as the robotic soft-tissue winner: ~100% share in approved procedures, 8,500 Da Vinci systems globally, and $7 billion revenue split 60% instruments / 25% systems. With 2.3 million procedures in 2023 and 14 million lifetime cases feeding algorithms, the moat is data plus surgeon trust — yet penetration is only ~10% of a 7-million-procedure addressable market.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Thomas's breakdown supports ISRG as a procedure-volume compounder with 70% gross margins, instrument-heavy revenue mix, and decade-long data moat — tempered by premium valuation at all-time highs and Ion competition from J&J in bronchoscopy.

episodes.title: Renaissance Technologies
episodes.conclusion: Renaissance Technologies — especially the Medallion fund — is the best-performing investment firm in history: ~39% net annualized over decades with no losing years after 1990, closed to outsiders since 1993, capped near ~$10B because edge decays with size. Jim Simons (codebreaker, Stony Brook builder) and mathematicians (Berlekamp, Ax, Mercer) found faint signals — right 50.75% of the time at huge frequency — not macro narratives. Medallion spun out of Axcom's hidden quant success while RenTech ran VC; Lenny Baum's bond blowup showed blow-up risk even here. Capacity limits forced 36–44% carry, then expelling external LPs in 2003. RIEF/RAM institutional products market lower-Sharpe 'not Medallion' beta. Lesson: edges are small, secret, and size-constrained — the opposite of Berkshire's public compounder model.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Medallion inaccessible. RIEF and quant peers offer different risk/return — capacity-limited alpha case study, not buyable ticker.

episodes.title: Hermès
episodes.conclusion: Hermès is the anti-LVMH: 187 years, sixth-generation Dumas family control, ~$230B market cap, yet still will not sell you a Birkin on demand. Thierry Hermès began as a harness maker (1837); saddlery, silk scarves (Robert Dumas whimsy), Kelly (1956), Birkin (1984) layered craft and art. Scarcity is strategy — waiting lists, artisan apprenticeship (~2 years), ~7,000 craftspeople, rejected manufacturing efficiencies. Bernard Arnault accumulated LVMH stake starting below $10B market cap; family poison-pill and unity repelled hostile control while going public (1993, 19% float). Veblen goods: demand rises with price; secondary Birkins exceed $500K. Jean-Louis Dumas saved Hermès from 1970s consultant 'be Gucci' era by honoring waitlists, not discounting. Axel Dumas CEO today — artistic (Pierre-Alexis) and business split preserved. Luxury strategy: never relocate factories.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Scarcity compounder at ~$230B with ~7% annual price increases. Monitor China luxury demand and any production pace increases signaling dilution.
LVMH remains diversified luxury hedge — Arnault proved trading Hermès stake profitable; portfolio model differs from Hermès purity play — pair for luxury exposure with different risk profiles.

episodes.title: Novo Nordisk (Ozempic)
episodes.conclusion: Novo Nordisk's Ozempic/Wegovy moment looks sudden but rests on a century of insulin rivalry, foundation control, and delivery-mechanism R&D. August Krogh (1920 Nobel) started Nordisk after his wife Marie's diabetes diagnosis; Pedersen brothers founded Novo down the street — WW2 scaled Novo across occupied Europe while Nordisk stayed Danish-small. Merger (1989) united scale for genetic-engineering capex; Novo Nordisk Foundation holds ~77% voting / ~28% economic — blocking hostile takeovers that would have killed the GLP-1 pipeline. Semaglutide trials began 2008; public woke in 2023. Eli Lilly competes; Danish pig/cow pancreas era to recombinant human insulin to once-weekly injections is one continuous competency. Pharma complexity (FDA, oligopolies, capex) makes this Acquired's hardest industry — yet Novo became ~15th largest company globally on obesity/diabetes convergence.

investmentIdeas.thesis: GLP-1 leadership with foundation governance; valuation embeds obesity boom. Watch Lilly tirzepatide, supply, payer pushback, patent lifecycle.
Eli Lilly's Mounjaro/Zepbound competes in same class with broader pharma diversification — GLP-1 share wars may cap monopoly rents for either duopolist.

episodes.title: Holiday Special 2023
episodes.conclusion: Acquired's 2023 holiday special is a state-of-the-franchise address: half a million listeners, Ben going full-time, and a format shift from 'technology acquisitions' to great companies broadly — only 4–6 of 14 season episodes were tech. LVMH and Costco changed how Ben and David think about scarcity, luxury anti-laws, and their own boutique throughput (~one episode/month). They retired generic 'specials' after data showed interviews underperformed season episodes — except Charlie Munger and Jensen Huang, which behaved like N-of-1 research products. Growth via YouTube (Porsche with Doug DeMuro) brought a different audience than the Slack community; sponsors and an Acquired-native investing flywheel (Vanta SPV) now fit scale. Visa ranked best episode of 2023; Nike was favorite but over-prepared. The through-line: have fun, laugh, remember you're good at this — NFL Sunday, not cram week.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Ben argues Spotify must find operating leverage beyond music's label economics — podcasting and audiobooks are rational bets; Acquired's 76% new-listener stat on Spotify shows distribution power, but profitability of scale podcasting remains unproven industry-wide.

episodes.title: Ferrari: Magic from Maranello
episodes.conclusion: Lum argues Ferrari is a luxury company miscategorized as an automaker: ~13,000 units against an 80-million-car industry, mid-high-20s EBIT margins, and a collector pyramid where two-thirds of new cars go to existing owners. The investment case is lifetime-value expansion — customization, limited editions like the 599-unit SP3 Daytona at $2M-plus, and a younger hybrid cohort via the 296 — not volume growth. Combinatorial brand, artisan manufacturing, and client gatekeeping create an edge no single-factor peer replicates.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Lum's framework: luxury margins on ~13,000 units, debt paid down post-2015 IPO, collector LTV expansion via customization and limited editions, global wealth creating new cohorts (40% of 296 buyers under 40), and Exor-aligned long-term brand stewardship — compounding slowly without volume inflection.

episodes.title: Visa
episodes.conclusion: Visa is the neutral railroad of commerce — born when Bank of America dropped 65,000 unsolicited BankAmericards on Fresno (1958), absorbed ~$20M fraud at 22% delinquency versus ~4% traditional lending, and proved scale beats caution. Franchisees revolted at 1970 Columbus summit; Dee Hock engineered non-stock membership coop — ownership tracks transaction share, cannot be sold, profits recycle pro-rata like NFL revenue share. IBANCO merged international operators; Visa name (1976) works globally unlike English-only BankAmericard. Interchange (~2% era) replaced check couriers. Moat: blue-gold-blue ubiquity mark on two-sided network racing to acceptance. Amex closed-loop competes on premium; Visa wins on openness. Among highest-margin large companies — not from lending, from moving bits and enforcing rules across thousands of competing member banks that cannot sell their stakes. Hock asked franchise banks: own a few percent of the default global commerce rail or 100% of BankAmericard — Visa chose ubiquity and became among the highest-margin large companies on Earth without taking credit balance-sheet risk on Visa Inc itself.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Visa remains the default open-loop rail — asset-light, no credit balance sheet, software-like margins on trillions of volume; regulation caps upside but ubiquity and duopoly with MA defend core economics.
Mastercard mirrors Visa's network coop origins; competitive parity on acceptance with regional strengths — pair trade or diversify payments exposure rather than bet on share shift.

episodes.title: Charlie Munger
episodes.conclusion: Ben and David's dinner with Charlie Munger — arranged by Andrew Marks at Charlie's Los Angeles home — is likely the only podcast Charlie ever recorded. The conversation is not a Berkshire history recap but a live application of Munger's mental models: Costco as a once-in-a-lifetime bet on low-SKU, capital-light retail; the virtue of partnerships that survive distance and decades; skepticism toward venture capital fee structures and crypto as a store of value; and admiration for Hermès-style craftsmanship alongside Kirkland Signature discipline. Charlie's BYD investment (~$270M to ~$8B) and his Salomon Brothers crisis framing that Berkshire's franchise was genuinely at risk when reputation mattered more than mark-to-market loss. At 99, he still preferred self-insurance, distrusted EBITDA, and argued capital has crowded into too few opportunities — a tension Acquired itself navigates as it scales.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Charlie's canonical compounder — membership economics, negative working capital, and cultural refusal to extract marginal margin align shareholders with customers; growth capped near ~10% by discipline, not demand, which he viewed as feature not bug.

Post-Charlie, Berkshire remains a concentrated capital-allocation vehicle (Apple, insurance float) but Charlie warned scale makes incremental deployment hard; monitor whether culture survives without his judgment on 'too hard' piles and franchise risk.

episodes.chronologySubject: Charlie Munger · Selected life & Berkshire moments

episodes.title: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang
episodes.conclusion: After ~500 hours researching Nvidia across three Acquired episodes, Ben and David sit down with Jensen Huang at headquarters — and still learn. Jensen's pattern: simulate the bet (RIVA 128, CUDA), then push all chips in when conviction is earned, not merely felt. CUDA succeeded because every Nvidia chip runs it — a militant platform choice born from losing to Microsoft on DirectX. The data-center arc (GeForce NOW, Mellanox/InfiniBand, DGX) was prerequisite to the AI explosion, not a side effect. Jensen runs 40+ direct reports tied to shipping missions, not hierarchy — enabling a shipping cadence Apple could not replicate. Platform moats beat tactics: outworking rivals helps, but CUDA plus systems integration is structural. At 30 years in, he still wakes up building accelerated computing; luck (1997 3D gaming inflection, AlexNet) met prepared infrastructure.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Platform moat (CUDA + systems) persists, but Jensen himself notes large swings and cyclical history; evaluate on data-center systems margin and developer lock-in, not single-product hype cycles — drawdowns have been 80%+ before.

episodes.title: Nvidia Part III: The Dawn of the AI Era (2022-2023)
episodes.guest: Nvidia Part III: The Dawn of the AI Era (2022-2023)
episodes.displaySubtitle: Part III: The Dawn of the AI Era (2022-2023) · Season 13 · Episode 3
episodes.conclusion: Nvidia Part III covers 2022–2023: crypto winter looked existential until ChatGPT proved LLMs need GPU supercomputers at scale. The Transformer (Google, 2017), scaling laws (sub-10B parameters useless; 100B+ 'magical'), and OpenAI's Microsoft-backed pivot created perfect demand for CUDA clusters. Jensen's non-AI bets paid off — Mellanox InfiniBand, Grace CPU, Hopper H100 ($40K/GPU), DGX systems ($150K–$300K+). Cloud access runs ~$100/hour for 8×H100 with scarce availability. Nvidia sells chips, bundles, and turnkey rows (256 racks, 'call us' pricing). Moritz corollary: human ambition expands to fill compute. Risk: hyperscaler custom silicon, export controls, cyclical history — but platform plus systems margin differs from prior booms.

investmentIdeas.thesis: AI era revenue is systems-plus-platform, not a one-quarter GPU spike — but valuation embeds perfection; monitor hyperscaler insourcing, China export rules, and supply normalization post-H100 crunch.
OpenAI exclusivity and Azure GPU capacity make Microsoft the enterprise distribution layer for generative AI — Nvidia hardware plus OpenAI models create a bundled cloud upsell competitors must match.

episodes.title: Nike
episodes.conclusion: Nike is the perfect Acquired case study for product vs marketing — answer: neither alone. A fabless ~$51B apparel giant (10% YoY growth) that manufactures zero shoes but dominates a ~$130B US athletic-footwear market it helped grow from ~$2B. Phil Knight's Blue Ribbon imported Tigers; Rob Strasser invented modern sports marketing; Michael Jordan saved the company and changed culture. Jordan Brand now ~$6B+ revenue growing ~35% — Jordan may earn $5–10B lifetime. Ben and David argue Nike's Seven Powers center on scale economies funding athlete 'Netflix shows' and $4B 'demand creation,' not classic brand pricing power (Dunks priced like Adidas). Strategic shift underway: digital/direct, 12-city marketing, NFL/NBA/MLB uniform deals paying for swooshes not jersey revenue. Air bags made in Oregon; Asia assembly. Trap: athletes hold wholesale transfer pricing power; Nike chooses volume of swooshes over $500 SKUs.

investmentIdeas.thesis: ~$51B revenue, ~10% growth, ~35% Jordan Brand growth, and scale-economy demand creation (~$4B) support durable lead vs Adidas — but apparel margins not software; athlete power, inventory ~$8.5B, and CEO transition cap multiple expansion; bull case is global swoosh ubiquity and basketball/sneakerhead strength.

Adidas Tegus interviews cite 'make brand cool again' — structural scale disadvantage vs Nike's athlete-halo spend; Portland Adidas America HQ legacy of Strasser poaching shows long competitive shadow but #2 position lacks Nike's uniform deals and Jordan-tier sub-brand.

episodes.title: Porsche (with Doug DeMuro)
episodes.conclusion: Doug DeMuro joins for Porsche — LVMH-level family drama with Nazi-era origins (Ferdinand designed Beetle for Hitler; Wolfsburg created). Post-war Ferry Porsche built 356 from supercharged Beetle roots (~$42K equivalent pricing); Louise/Austria dealership empire and VW royalty deal (paid on every Beetle globally) funded R&D when Germany taxed profits at ~95%. Wiedeking's 1990s turnaround (~€400M to ~€32B market cap by 2007) ended in 2008 VW-option squeeze disaster — Porsche SE loaded ~$10B debt buying VW shares; VW reversed and bought Porsche operating co for ~€8.5B. Sept 2022 Porsche AG re-IPO: largest European IPO, ~$75B opening cap (~$115B nine months later) — marketing securities more than creating operational value per Ben. Today ~$40B+ revenue, ~two-thirds SUVs (Cayenne/Macan), ~29% gross margin vs BMW ~17%, Ferrari ~48%. Porsche is usable luxury (Rolex/Rimowa) not garage queen.

investmentIdeas.thesis: Porsche AG post-2022 IPO trades premium brand + SUV cash generation (~29% gross margin, ~$40B revenue) — EV transition (Taycan) and China 26% exposure key variables; structurally tied to VW Group supply chain and family governance via Porsche SE.

Porsche/Piech families control ~32% VW voting post-2008 reverse bailout — Porsche AG re-IPO clarified sum-of-parts but operating integration remains; 2008 squeeze history shows cross-holding complexity dominates pure automotive fundamentals.

episodes.title: Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi
episodes.conclusion: Four years after Acquired's IPO-day Uber episode, Dara Khosrowshahi visits Seattle to show a different company: ~$30B trailing revenue vs ~$10B two years prior, divested speculative bets, and profitability where Uber once burned more pre-IPO cash than any company in history (~$3B the year before listing). The interview is half Uber strategy — cross-marketing mobility and Eats, marketplace pricing discipline, food-delivery competition — and half Dara's arc from Allen & Company to Barry Diller's IAC, Expedia CEO (2004–2017), and the secret CEO search that installed him amid boardroom chaos. His lesson from Booking.com: geographic density and execution machines beat narrative.
